chapters.title: Who Is Allah?
chapters.subtitle: The one God who made everything and loves you

pages.text: Who Is Allah?
This whole book is about Allah. “Allah” is simply the name of God. The two words mean the same thing.
Long before the earth, the sea, or the very first morning, there was Allah. He has no beginning.
Nothing made Allah, and nothing is older than Him. He has always been, and He always will be.
Everything else — every star, every sea, every living thing — was made by Him.

interaction.prompt: Tap three signs that point back to the One who made them.

markers.label: Stars
markers.reveal: The stars are signs, not gods; Allah made their light.

markers.label: Sea
markers.reveal: The sea is vast, yet it obeys the limits Allah gives it.

markers.label: Life
markers.reveal: Every living thing is a gift from Allah, the Creator.

interaction.completeText: Creation becomes a quiet lesson when you know who made it.

arabic.meaning: The Maker — the One who creates everything out of nothing.

pages.text: To make something, Allah does not need tools or time. He simply says to it, “Be,” and it is.

quote.translation: When He decrees a thing, He only says to it, “Be,” and it is.

pages.text: There is only one God. Not many gods — only one. He has no partner and no equal.
Allah was not born, and He has no mother, father, or children. Nothing is like Him in any way.
He has no body and no shape. Our eyes cannot see Him, the way they can see the sun.

interaction.prompt: What does it mean that Allah is not like creation?

options.label: He has a body like ours.
options.feedback: Allah is not a created body, shape, or thing.

options.label: Nothing is like Him.
options.feedback: Yes. Allah is real, near, and unlike anything He made.

options.label: He is only a hidden star.
options.feedback: Stars are created things. Allah made every star.

interaction.completeText: You kept the most important boundary clear.

pages.text: Yet He sees everything, and nothing is hidden from Him — not even a quiet thought inside you.

arabic.meaning: The one God — the Maker of all things. “Allah” and “God” mean the same.

pages.text: Allah gave people a book of His own words, to tell them the truth about Him. It is called the Qur’an.
In it, Allah taught a short, beautiful chapter that tells us exactly who He is.

quote.translation: Say, “He is Allah, who is One. Allah, the Eternal. He neither gives birth nor was born, and there is none equal to Him.”

pages.text: We cannot see Allah with our eyes. But everywhere we look, He has left signs that He is real.
A sign is something that points to something else — the way footprints point to the one who walked.
Look up by day: the sun rises right on time, warms your face, and sets — every single day.
Look up by night: countless stars, and a moon that grows and fades in a steady, faithful rhythm.
See the mountains, standing firm and still, holding the earth steady beneath your feet.
See the wide sea, brimming with water and life, never once spilling past its shore.
Allah sends the rain from the sky, and the dry, brown land wakes up soft and green and alive.

interaction.prompt: Tap what changes when Allah sends rain.

markers.label: Rain
markers.reveal: Rain is rizq: provision Allah sends from above.

markers.label: Earth
markers.reveal: Dry earth can look finished, but Allah can wake it again.

markers.label: Green life
markers.reveal: New shoots remind us that Allah brings life from what seems still.

interaction.completeText: Rain turns the page from empty to alive.

quote.translation: ...and We made every living thing from water. Will they not then believe?

pages.text: From one tiny seed He grows a great tree, with cool shade for birds and sweet fruit for you.
He fills the sky with birds and the fields with animals, and He feeds and cares for every one.
And He made you — your eyes, your breath, and the small, steady heart beating in your chest right now.
None of this made itself. Each thing is a gift — and every gift points back to the Giver.

interaction.prompt: Put the lesson of the signs in order.
interaction.completeText: That is the path from wonder to gratitude.

quote.translation: So which of the favors of your Lord would you deny?

pages.text: Allah has many beautiful names. Each one is a little window that shows us something true about Him.
He is the Most Merciful. Mercy means gentle, loving care — even more than a mother gives her child.

arabic.meaning: Mercy — Allah’s endless love, gentleness, and care for you.

pages.text: He is the Most Forgiving. When we do wrong and feel sorry, He loves to forgive us and start again.

interaction.prompt: When someone does wrong and feels sorry, what does Allah love?

options.label: Hide forever.
options.feedback: Hiding keeps the heart heavy. Allah invites us back.

options.label: Return and ask.
options.feedback: Yes. Allah loves to forgive the one who turns back.

options.label: Pretend nothing happened.
options.feedback: A truthful heart does not need pretending.

interaction.completeText: Mercy is not far away.

pages.text: He is the Provider. The food you eat and the warm home you sleep in are all gifts from Him.

interaction.prompt: Tap gifts that teach Allah is the Provider.

markers.label: Dates
markers.reveal: Food reaches us because Allah provides through the earth and people.

markers.label: Bread
markers.reveal: A simple meal can carry a big lesson of gratitude.

markers.label: Warmth
markers.reveal: Home, warmth, and safety are also provisions from Allah.

interaction.completeText: Provision is not only food; it is care.

pages.text: He is the All-Knowing. He knows everything that was, everything that is, and all that is still to come.
He is the Most Wise and the Most Fair. He never wrongs anyone, not even by the weight of a seed.

quote.translation: In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. All praise is for Allah, Lord of all the worlds.

pages.text: Allah is not far away and out of reach. He is closer to you than you can even imagine.
He hears you even when you whisper — even when you only speak quietly inside your own heart.
You do not need a special place or special words. You can call on Him anywhere, at any time.
He never grows tired of listening, and He is never, ever too busy for you.

quote.translation: And when My servants ask you about Me — indeed I am near. I respond to the call of the one who calls upon Me.

interaction.prompt: What should you remember when you call on Allah?

options.label: He is too busy.
options.feedback: Allah never grows tired and is never too busy.

options.label: He is near.
options.feedback: Yes. Allah hears even a whisper inside the heart.

options.label: Only special places count.
options.feedback: You can call on Allah anywhere.

interaction.completeText: Dua can begin anywhere.

pages.text: Talking to Allah is called prayer. It can be as simple as “O Allah, help me,” or just “thank You.”

arabic.meaning: A call to God — talking to Allah and asking Him for what you need.

pages.text: Allah did not need to make you. He made you because He wanted to — as a gift of His love.
He made you to know Him, to love Him, and to live with kindness, honesty, and gratitude.
So that people would not forget Him, Allah chose certain humans to remind everyone of the truth.
These chosen humans are called prophets. A prophet is a person who carries God’s message to others.
Allah sent many prophets — Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus — and last of all, Muhammad.
Every one of them taught the same simple truth: there is one God — so love Him, and be good.
This book will tell you their stories, one by one. They are some of the best stories ever lived.

interaction.prompt: Order the way Allah guides people in this book.
interaction.completeText: Every story is part of one bright message.

pages.text: If you forget everything else, remember this: Allah made you, Allah knows you, and Allah loves you.
His mercy is bigger than any mistake, wider than the whole sky, and deeper than the deepest sea.

quote.translation: ...My mercy encompasses all things.

pages.text: He is waiting for you to turn to Him — and He is so happy whenever you do.

arabic.meaning: All praise and thanks belong to Allah — said for every good thing.

chapters.title: What Is the Qur’an?
chapters.subtitle: Allah’s own words, sent to guide the world

pages.text: What Is the Qur’an?
You have read many books. People write them — their own thoughts, their own stories. The Qur’an is different.
Muslims believe the Qur’an is not a person’s book at all. Every word in it is the word of Allah, the one God.

interaction.prompt: Why is the Qur'an different from an ordinary book?

options.label: People invented it.
options.feedback: Muslims believe the Qur'an is Allah's words, not invented by people.

options.label: It is Allah's words.
options.feedback: Yes. That is why Muslims treat it with love and care.

options.label: It is only decoration.
options.feedback: The Qur'an is recited, learned, remembered, and lived.

interaction.completeText: The Qur'an is a guide, not just a book on a shelf.

pages.text: Allah sent it down to people to guide them, to comfort them, and to tell them the truth about everything.
It is the most read, the most memorized, and one of the most loved books in the whole world.

arabic.meaning: “The Recitation” — Allah’s words, meant to be read aloud and remembered.

pages.text: The word “Qur’an” means “the Recitation” — because it is meant to be spoken aloud, not just kept on a shelf.

quote.translation: This is the Book about which there is no doubt, a guidance for those mindful of Allah.

pages.text: About 1,400 years ago, in the deserts of Arabia, there lived a man named Muhammad, known for his honesty.
He would often go alone to a quiet cave in the mountains to sit, and to think about God and the world.
One night, when he was forty years old, the cave suddenly filled with light, and an angel appeared.
An angel is a being of light that Allah creates to carry out His commands. This angel was named Gabriel.
Gabriel brought the very first words of the Qur’an. His first command to Muhammad was simple: “Read.”

quote.translation: Read! In the name of your Lord who created — created the human being from a clinging clot.

pages.text: Muhammad could not read or write a single word — yet he remembered everything Gabriel gave him, perfectly.
The Qur’an did not arrive all at once. It came down a little at a time, over twenty-three years.

interaction.prompt: Put the first revelation story in order.
interaction.completeText: The Qur'an came with care, over time.

pages.text: Piece by piece, Allah’s words answered people’s questions and guided them through every kind of day.
The Qur’an was given in the Arabic language, and it is recited aloud in a flowing, almost musical way.

interaction.prompt: Tap the parts of the Qur'an's sound-life.

markers.label: Book
markers.reveal: The Qur'an is kept in pages, but it is meant to be recited.

markers.label: Voice
markers.reveal: Recitation lets the words be heard by the heart.

markers.label: Light
markers.reveal: Its meaning guides like a light in a dark room.

interaction.completeText: The Qur'an lives in reading, listening, and remembering.

pages.text: Its sound is so beautiful that even people who do not speak Arabic have wept simply from listening.
When it is recited, many feel a deep calm, as if the words are speaking straight into their heart.

arabic.meaning: A verse of the Qur’an — the same word also means a ‘sign’ of Allah.

pages.text: Each small piece of the Qur’an is called an ayah. The very same word means a “sign” — like the signs in creation.
A whole group of ayahs together is called a chapter. The Qur’an has 114 chapters in all.
Each chapter has a name — like “The Opening,” “The Light,” or “Mary” — names we will meet in these stories.
Allah Himself promised to protect the Qur’an, so that it could never be lost or changed.

quote.translation: Indeed, it is We who sent down the Reminder, and indeed, We will be its guardian.

pages.text: It was kept safe in two ways at once: written down with great care, and memorized by heart.

interaction.prompt: Tap the two ways the Qur'an was kept safe.

markers.label: Written
markers.reveal: Careful writing helped preserve the words.

markers.label: Heart
markers.reveal: Memorizing by heart made the Qur'an travel inside people.

markers.label: Care
markers.reveal: The words were guarded with reverence from the beginning.

interaction.completeText: Written lines and living hearts worked together.

pages.text: From the very start, people wrote down each new verse and learned it, word for word, by heart.
Allah made the Qur’an easy to remember, so that millions of people could carry it inside them.

quote.translation: And We have certainly made the Qur’an easy to remember. So is there anyone who will be reminded?

pages.text: A person who memorizes the entire Qur’an is called a hafiz — a “keeper” of Allah’s words.
Even today, young children become huffaz, able to recite the whole book aloud from memory.
Because of this, the Qur’an today is exactly the same as it was 1,400 years ago — not one word changed.
So what does the Qur’an actually say? First of all, it tells us who Allah is, and how much He loves us.
It is full of the stories of the prophets — Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Mary, Jesus, and many more.
It teaches us how to live: to be honest, kind, patient, fair, and grateful in everything we do.
It comforts the sad, gives hope to the worried, and reminds us that we are never, ever alone.
And it points to Allah’s signs all around us — the sky, the rain, the sea, and even our own selves.
Everything in it leads to the same place: knowing Allah, and living the way that He loves.
Because the Qur’an is Allah’s own words, Muslims treat it with great care and deep respect.

interaction.prompt: Why do Muslims treat the Qur'an with care?

options.label: Because paper is rare.
options.feedback: The paper matters less than whose words it carries.

options.label: Because it is Allah's words.
options.feedback: Yes. Love for Allah makes the reader careful.

options.label: Because it is for display.
options.feedback: The Qur'an is loved most by being read and lived.

interaction.completeText: Respect grows from knowing what the Qur'an is.

pages.text: They keep it clean and held up high, open it gently, and quiet their hearts before they read.
Many Muslims read a little of it every single day, and recite its verses inside their prayers.
Children learn it slowly, a few verses at a time, often by lamplight beside someone they love.

interaction.prompt: Tap how children slowly learn the Qur'an.

markers.label: Teacher
markers.reveal: A trusted teacher helps the words be learned correctly.

markers.label: Pages
markers.reveal: A few ayahs at a time can become a lifetime of guidance.

markers.label: Home
markers.reveal: The Qur'an is often learned beside someone who loves you.

interaction.completeText: Small lessons can grow into a whole book carried by heart.

pages.text: The more they read it, the more they understand — it is a book you can keep growing up inside.
You do not need to know Arabic to begin. You can start with its meaning, and with its stories.

interaction.prompt: If you do not know Arabic yet, where can you begin?

options.label: Wait until you know everything.
options.feedback: You can begin before you know everything.

options.label: Begin with meaning and stories.
options.feedback: Yes. Understanding can start gently, step by step.

options.label: Ignore the Qur'an.
options.feedback: The stories are an open door, not a closed one.

interaction.completeText: A first step still counts.

pages.text: That is what this whole book is — some of the most beautiful stories from the Qur’an, told just for you.
As you read them, remember: these are not just tales. They are Allah’s gift, sent to guide you home.

arabic.meaning: “In the name of Allah” — said before reading, eating, or starting anything good.

chapters.title: Prophet Muhammad ﷺ
chapters.subtitle: The life and message of the final prophet

pages.text: Prophet Muhammad ﷺ
This is the story of a man named Muhammad, and of the message he carried to the whole world.
To understand him, we begin with one simple idea: there is one God who made everything that exists.

arabic.meaning: The one God — the Maker of all things. "Allah" and "God" mean the same.

pages.text: Muslims believe God has no shape and no equal. We cannot see Him, yet all around us are signs that He is there.
God made people to know Him and to be good — kind, honest, and grateful for all He gives.
So people would not forget Him, God chose certain humans to remind them. These messengers are called prophets.

arabic.meaning: A prophet — a person God chooses to carry His message to others.

pages.text: A prophet is not a god. He is a human who lives and sleeps like us — but he speaks the words God gives him.

interaction.prompt: Which sentence describes a prophet?

options.label: A prophet is a god.
options.feedback: A prophet is human, not God.

options.label: A human with God's message.
options.feedback: Yes. A prophet lives as a human and carries God's words.

options.label: Only a powerful king.
options.feedback: Some prophets were kings, but many were not.

interaction.completeText: That keeps love for prophets in the right place.

pages.text: There were many prophets before — Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus among them. Muhammad was the last.
His life still shapes how more than a billion people pray, give, and treat one another today.

section.title: The World He Was Born Into

pages.text: About 1,400 years ago lay a vast, dry land of sand and stone — the desert of Arabia.
Today we call this land Saudi Arabia. Back then it had few roads and little water — only towns and wandering tribes.
In the heart of it stood a town called Mecca, a resting place for traders crossing the sands.
At the center of Mecca was a simple cube-shaped house of stone, called the Kaaba.

interaction.prompt: Tap three parts of Muhammad's early world.

markers.label: Kaaba
markers.reveal: The Kaaba stood at the center of Mecca.

markers.label: Hills
markers.reveal: Mecca sat in a dry valley between bare hills.

markers.label: Traders
markers.reveal: Travelers and traders passed through the town.

interaction.completeText: The message began in a real place.

pages.text: Long before, the prophet Abraham had built it as a place to worship the one God.

arabic.meaning: The Kaaba — the cube-shaped house of worship at the center of Mecca.

pages.text: But over the years people forgot. They filled the Kaaba with idols — statues they prayed to instead of God.
The people of Mecca lived in tribes — large families and clans who often quarreled and fought.
The strong took from the weak. The poor were ignored, and many people were bought and sold as slaves.
Into this troubled world, in the year 570, a baby boy was born.
His name was Muhammad, which means "the praised one."

arabic.meaning: "The praised one" — the name of the final prophet.

pages.text: His father had died before he was born. When Muhammad was six years old, his mother died too.
He was an orphan — a child with no mother or father to raise him.
His grandfather cared for him, and then his kind uncle, Abu Talib, took him in.
As a boy, Muhammad worked as a shepherd, watching over sheep in the hills outside Mecca.
He grew into a young man known for one thing above all: he never lied and never cheated anyone.
People trusted him so completely that they nicknamed him Al-Amin — "the trustworthy one."

interaction.prompt: What did Al-Amin mean in daily life?

options.label: The loudest one.
options.feedback: Trust was not about being loud.

options.label: The trustworthy one.
options.feedback: Yes. People trusted Muhammad because he was honest.

options.label: The richest one.
options.feedback: His honor was honesty, not wealth.

interaction.completeText: Trust is a beautiful kind of strength.

pages.text: We will call him Muhammad — but to everyone who knew him, he was simply the one you could trust.
A wise, successful merchant named Khadijah saw his honesty and hired him to lead her caravans.
She came to admire him deeply, and they married. Khadijah was the first to believe in him.
One year the people of Mecca repaired the Kaaba. But when the work was done, they began to argue.
Each tribe wanted the honor of lifting the holy Black Stone back into the Kaaba's corner.
They nearly came to blows. So they agreed: the next person to arrive would decide. It was Muhammad.
He laid his cloak on the ground, placed the stone in its center, and called a leader from every tribe.

interaction.prompt: Tap the pieces of Muhammad's peaceful solution.

markers.label: Cloak
markers.reveal: The cloak let everyone share the honor.

markers.label: Stone
markers.reveal: The Black Stone was lifted without one tribe taking all the pride.

markers.label: Hands
markers.reveal: Many leaders carried together, so anger cooled.

interaction.completeText: A wise answer made room for everyone.

pages.text: No one was left out, and no one was first. Even young, Muhammad turned anger into peace.

section.title: The Cave and the First Words

pages.text: As Muhammad grew older, the unfairness of Mecca weighed on his heart — the idols, the cruelty, the forgotten poor.
He began to go alone to a cave high on a mountain near Mecca — to sit, to think, and to pray.

arabic.meaning: The cave on the Mountain of Light where Muhammad went to pray.

pages.text: He was now a grown man of forty. One night, in the quiet dark of the cave, everything changed.
A being of light filled the cave — an angel. Angels are beings God makes from light to carry out His will.

arabic.meaning: An angel — a being of light created by God to serve Him and deliver His messages.

pages.text: This was Gabriel, the mightiest angel — the one God sends to bring His words to His prophets.
Gabriel had come to prophets before — to Abraham, to Moses, to Mary, the mother of Jesus.
"Read!" the angel commanded. "But I cannot read," Muhammad answered, for he had never been taught.

interaction.prompt: Tap what is happening in the cave.

markers.label: Light
markers.reveal: Gabriel is shown with light, bringing Allah's words.

markers.label: Cave
markers.reveal: This is Hira, the quiet place where revelation began.

markers.label: Muhammad
markers.reveal: Muhammad is shown respectfully without a face.

interaction.completeText: The first words arrived in awe and mercy.

pages.text: The command came again, and then — by God's will — words rose to his lips that he had never known.

arabic.meaning: "Read" or "Recite" — the very first word God revealed to Muhammad.

quote.translation: Read in the name of your Lord who created — created man from a clinging clot. Read, and your Lord is the Most Generous, who taught by the pen — taught man what he did not know.

pages.text: These were the very first words of the Qur'an, the holy book God would reveal to him.
Shaking with awe, Muhammad hurried home. Khadijah wrapped him warmly and said God would not forsake him.
From that night, God's words came to Muhammad little by little, over twenty-three years.
All these words together are called the Qur'an. The name means "the recitation" — words read aloud.

arabic.meaning: The Qur'an — God's words given to Muhammad, the holy book of Islam.

pages.text: Muhammad did not write the Qur'an. He could not read or write — he only spoke the words God gave him.
His companions wrote the words down, and many people learned the entire Qur'an by heart.
Even today, millions of people have memorized every word — unchanged for fourteen hundred years.
Muslims believe the Qur'an is God speaking to us — guiding how to live, to be just, and to love.
Its words are also beautiful to hear. Their rhythm alone can move a listener to tears.
Muhammad began to teach what God had told him. His message was simple, but it changed the world.
First: there is only one God. Stop praying to idols of stone, and worship the Maker of all alone.

interaction.prompt: What was the first message Muhammad taught?

options.label: Pray to many gods.
options.feedback: That was exactly what he came to correct.

options.label: Worship Allah alone.
options.feedback: Yes. This is tawhid: Allah is one.

options.label: Your tribe is greatest.
options.feedback: He taught goodness, not tribe pride.

interaction.completeText: The message was simple and world-changing.

arabic.meaning: Believing in and worshiping one God alone — the heart of Islam.

pages.text: He taught people to be honest, to keep their promises, and to treat one another fairly.
He told the rich to care for the poor, the orphan, and the traveler who had nothing.

interaction.prompt: Tap three people or needs Muhammad taught us to care for.

markers.label: Food
markers.reveal: Feeding others is mercy you can hold in your hands.

markers.label: Poor
markers.reveal: The poor and traveler were not to be forgotten.

markers.label: Hands
markers.reveal: Helping hands turn belief into action.

interaction.completeText: Mercy becomes real when it helps someone.

pages.text: He taught that women were to be honored, not mistreated — and that daughters were a blessing.
He urged people to free those they had enslaved, and to be gentle even with animals.
And no one, he taught, is better for their tribe or the color of their skin — only for their goodness.
But the powerful men of Mecca grew angry. His message threatened their idols and their pride.
Muhammad and his followers were mocked and harmed, and yet he answered cruelty with patience.
At last they left Mecca for a city to the north called Medina, where the people welcomed them.

arabic.meaning: The migration of Muhammad and his followers from Mecca to Medina.

pages.text: This journey, in the year 622, was so important that it begins the Muslim calendar.
In Medina he built the first mosque, and a community where neighbors truly cared for one another.
Muhammad taught that he brought no brand-new religion — he carried the same message as the prophets before him.
The same God who spoke to Muhammad had also spoken to Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus.
Each prophet told their people the same truth: there is one God, so be good and worship Him alone.
Muslims love and honor every one of these prophets. To insult any of them is not allowed.
Muhammad was the last of them — the final messenger, sent to all people, everywhere, for all time.
God told Muhammad the very reason he was sent — and the reason was love.

quote.translation: And We have not sent you except as a mercy to all the worlds.

arabic.meaning: Mercy — gentleness and compassion. Muhammad was sent as a mercy to all.

pages.text: Muslims try to live the way he lived: honest, generous, patient, and kind to every creature.
When they pray, give to the poor, or smile at a stranger, they are following his example.
His last words to his people urged them to be kind to one another and to never forget God.
Today more than a billion people across every land love him and try to walk his path.
And now you know how it began — with one honest man, one quiet cave, and one word: "Read."

interaction.prompt: Put Muhammad's story arc in order.
interaction.completeText: You followed the path from honesty to mercy.

chapters.title: Adam
chapters.subtitle: The first human, and the gift of turning back

pages.text: Adam
When the world was still young, Allah decided to make a new kind of being — one who could think, choose, and love.
From the soft clay of the earth, Allah shaped the very first human with His own care.

interaction.prompt: Tap what Adam's creation teaches.

markers.label: Clay
markers.reveal: Adam began from earth, shaped by Allah's care.

markers.label: Life
markers.reveal: Life and soul are gifts from Allah.

markers.label: Earth
markers.reveal: The first human connects every family back to one beginning.

interaction.completeText: Human life is honored from its first moment.

pages.text: Then Allah gave him life, and breath, and a soul — something living and precious inside.
Allah also gave people something special: the freedom to choose. Because we can choose, our good choices truly count.
His name was Adam. He was the first of all people, and the father of everyone who would ever live — including you.
Allah loved Adam, and taught him something no other creature knew — the names of all things.
Every animal, every plant, every star — Adam learned what each one was, and what to call it.
To know the name of a thing is to understand it. Allah made people who could learn, and think, and grow wise.

quote.translation: And He taught Adam the names — all of them. Then He showed them to the angels and said, “Tell Me the names of these, if you are truthful.”

pages.text: The angels said, “We only know what You have taught us.” But Adam knew the names — and Allah had honored him.
An angel is a being of light that Allah creates to serve Him. Allah told all the angels to bow to Adam, to honor him.
Every single angel bowed at once — except one proud creature named Satan, who stubbornly refused.
“I am better than him,” Satan said. He was full of pride — thinking himself greater than he was.

interaction.prompt: What made Satan's heart cold?

options.label: Asking questions.
options.feedback: Good questions can lead to truth.

options.label: Pride.
options.feedback: Yes. Pride made him think he was too great to obey.

options.label: Being small.
options.feedback: Smallness is not the problem. Pride is.

interaction.completeText: Pride pushes the heart away from mercy.

pages.text: Pride is a heavy thing. It carried Satan far from Allah’s mercy. Pride is the first thing that turns a heart cold.
Satan could have simply said, “I am sorry.” But his pride would not let him bend. Remember that — it matters very soon.
From that day, Satan promised to whisper to people, to try to pull them away from the good. But he can only whisper — never force.
Allah gave Adam a wife named Eve, so the two would never be alone. They lived together in a beautiful Garden.
The Garden was full of every delight — sweet fruit, clear streams, soft shade, and perfect peace.
Allah told them, “Enjoy all of this freely — but do not go near that one tree.” Just one was set apart.

interaction.prompt: Tap the gifts and the one boundary in the Garden.

markers.label: Fruit
markers.reveal: The Garden was full of gifts Adam and Eve could enjoy.

markers.label: Streams
markers.reveal: Allah surrounded them with beauty and peace.

markers.label: One tree
markers.reveal: One tree was a gentle test of trust.

interaction.completeText: Love can be shown by keeping one promise.

pages.text: It was a small, gentle test. When someone trusts you, keeping one promise shows that you love them back.
Then Satan began to whisper. “Wouldn’t you like to stay here forever? Just taste that tree,” he said, again and again.
That is how a whisper works: it makes something wrong look harmless, even nice — until you forget why you said no.
He whispered so long that at last they forgot Allah’s words — and they ate from the one tree.
The very moment they did, the joy drained away. They felt sorrow — they knew they had done wrong.
But here is the beautiful part: they did not blame anyone else, and they did not run away from Allah.

interaction.prompt: What did Adam and Eve do after their mistake?

options.label: Blame someone else.
options.feedback: They did not hide behind blame.

options.label: Turned back to Allah.
options.feedback: Yes. They asked Allah for mercy with humble hearts.

options.label: Ran away forever.
options.feedback: Running away keeps the wound open.

interaction.completeText: Tawbah begins by turning back.

pages.text: Adam and Eve turned straight to Allah, and with all their hearts they asked Him to forgive them.

quote.translation: Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers.

pages.text: And Allah, the Most Forgiving, welcomed them back at once. He was not waiting to punish — He was waiting to forgive.
See the difference: Satan disobeyed and grew proud. Adam disobeyed and grew humble. Humility is what brought him home.
Turning back to Allah after a mistake has a name: tawbah. Allah loves it when His people turn back to Him.

arabic.meaning: Turning back to Allah — He always welcomes a sorry, hopeful heart.

pages.text: Allah sent Adam and Eve from the Garden to live on the earth, and from them came all the families of the world.

interaction.prompt: Put Adam's story in order.
interaction.completeText: The story moves from honor to mercy.

pages.text: Everyone makes mistakes — even the very first people did. Making a mistake is not the end of the story.
This is Adam’s first gift to us all: when you slip, you do not run from Allah — you run to Him.

interaction.prompt: When you slip, where does Adam teach you to run?

options.label: Away from Allah.
options.feedback: That only makes the heart lonelier.

options.label: To Allah.
options.feedback: Yes. His mercy is the safest place to go.

interaction.completeText: A mistake is not the end of the story.

pages.text: His door is always open, His mercy always near. No mistake is too big for Him to forgive.

chapters.title: Noah
chapters.subtitle: The patient prophet and the great ark

pages.text: Noah
Many, many years after Adam, people slowly began to forget the One who had made them.
Instead of Allah, they began bowing to idols — statues of stone they had carved with their own hands.
An idol cannot see, or hear, or help anyone. Yet the people prayed to them, and forgot the true God.
So Allah, in His mercy, sent the people a gentle and patient teacher — a prophet named Noah.
His message was simple and kind: “Worship Allah alone. He is the One who made you and who loves you.”
Noah taught his people for a very long time — longer than you can imagine, year after year after year.
Morning and evening he called them back, always gentle, never giving up, never growing unkind.
A few people listened, and believed. But most only laughed at him, and turned away.
Still Noah did not stop. Staying gentle and hopeful through long, hard days has a name: patience.

interaction.prompt: What did Noah's patience look like?

options.label: He quit quickly.
options.feedback: Noah kept calling with hope.

options.label: He stayed gentle.
options.feedback: Yes. Sabr means holding on to good without becoming cruel.

options.label: He mocked them back.
options.feedback: He answered mockery with patience.

interaction.completeText: Patience can be strong and soft at the same time.

arabic.meaning: Patience — staying gentle and hopeful through long, hard days.

pages.text: At last Allah gave Noah a surprising command: “Build a great ship” — far away from any sea.
The people pointed and giggled: “A boat? On dry land? How silly!” But Noah trusted Allah, and began.

quote.translation: And construct the ship under Our eyes and Our inspiration...

pages.text: Day after day, Noah and the believers cut and shaped the wood, while the others mocked from far away.

interaction.prompt: Tap what Noah kept doing while others laughed.

markers.label: Wood
markers.reveal: Every plank was an act of trust.

markers.label: Work
markers.reveal: Faith can look like steady work, day after day.

markers.label: Mockers
markers.reveal: Noah did not let laughter stop obedience.

interaction.completeText: The ark was built with patience before the rain ever came.

pages.text: To trust Allah even when it looks strange to everyone else — that is what real faith looks like.

interaction.prompt: Why did building a ship on dry land make sense?

options.label: Everyone liked the plan.
options.feedback: Most people laughed at it.

options.label: Allah commanded it.
options.feedback: Yes. Allah's command was safer than people's laughter.

options.label: Noah guessed wildly.
options.feedback: Noah was following revelation, not guessing.

interaction.completeText: Trust listens higher than the crowd.

pages.text: When the great ship was ready, Allah told Noah to bring aboard a pair of every kind of animal.

interaction.prompt: Tap who came into the ark.

markers.label: Animals
markers.reveal: Allah gathered the creatures in pairs.

markers.label: Ark
markers.reveal: The ark became safety because Allah made it safety.

markers.label: Believers
markers.reveal: Those who trusted Allah climbed aboard together.

interaction.completeText: The rescue gathered people and creatures under Allah's care.

pages.text: Two by two they came — elephants and deer, rabbits and doves — calm and orderly, as if they understood.
And all the people who believed climbed aboard too, safe together inside the warm wooden hull.
Then the sky opened. Rain poured down, and water rose up from the earth, until the whole land was covered.
The great ark lifted gently from the ground and floated, riding safely on the rising water.
As it set off, Noah said, “In the name of Allah it sails, and in His name it comes to rest.”

quote.translation: And he said, “Embark therein; in the name of Allah is its course and its anchorage. Indeed, my Lord is Forgiving and Merciful.”

pages.text: Noah was not afraid, because he knew the most important thing of all: Allah was with them.

interaction.prompt: What truly carried the ark through the flood?

options.label: Wood by itself.
options.feedback: The wood mattered, but Allah was the true Protector.

options.label: Allah was with them.
options.feedback: Yes. That is why Noah was not afraid.

interaction.completeText: Safety comes from Allah.

pages.text: Inside, safe and dry, the people and the animals waited together while the storm raged outside.
They trusted Allah and stayed calm, listening to the soft drum of the rain on the wooden roof.
At last the rain stopped. The waters sank away. The ark came gently to rest upon a mountain.

interaction.prompt: Put the flood story in order.
interaction.completeText: You steered the story safely to dry land.

pages.text: A fresh, clean, green world lay waiting — washed new, and ready to begin all over again.
Noah and the believers stepped out onto the soft grass and thanked Allah with all their hearts.

arabic.meaning: Gratitude — saying thank you to Allah from a full heart.

pages.text: Noah teaches us to be patient — to keep doing good even when it is slow, and even when others laugh.
He teaches us to trust Allah, even when His plan looks strange to everyone around us.
And he teaches us to say thank you — to remember the One who carried us safely through the storm.
However few believe, and however long it takes, Allah never forgets the ones who hold on to Him.
Abraham
Long ago, in a town of stone and dust, the people worshipped statues they carved with their own hands.
They gave the idols food, and knelt before them, and called them gods. But the statues could not hear, or see, or help.
A boy named Abraham watched all of this and wondered. He asked the biggest question anyone could ask: who really made the world?
His father and his people said, "These are our gods." But Abraham felt something stirring in his heart — the statues were just stone.

interaction.prompt: Why did Abraham doubt the idols?

options.label: They were only stone.
options.feedback: Yes. They could not hear, see, create, or help.

options.label: They were too small.
options.feedback: Size was not the problem. Being powerless was.

options.label: They were too old.
options.feedback: Age did not make them gods.

interaction.completeText: A made thing cannot be the Maker.

section.title: The Stars, the Moon, and the Sun

pages.text: One night, Abraham looked up at the sky full of stars. So many lights — so beautiful. "Could one of these be my Lord?" he wondered.
But as the night turned, the stars faded and were gone. They were not the greatest thing — they were only lights that pass.
Then the moon rose, round and silver. "Surely this is greater!" he thought. But by morning, the moon had set too.
Then the sun blazed up — the brightest thing he had ever seen. "This must be the greatest!" But by evening, even the sun sank below the hills.
Every beautiful thing in the sky passes away. Abraham understood: the One who made all of them never sets and never fades.

interaction.prompt: Tap what Abraham learned from the sky.

markers.label: Stars
markers.reveal: The stars were beautiful, but they faded.

markers.label: Moon
markers.reveal: The moon was greater, but it also set.

markers.label: Horizon
markers.reveal: The Creator of the sky never sets.

interaction.completeText: The signs led Abraham beyond the signs.

quote.translation: Indeed, I have turned my face toward He who created the heavens and the earth, inclining toward truth, and I am not of those who associate others with Allah.

arabic.meaning: Believing in only One God — Allah alone, with no partners.

pages.text: Abraham asked his people gently: "Why do you worship things that cannot hear you, cannot help you, and cannot even move?"
They would not listen. So when the townspeople went away for a festival, brave Abraham went alone to the idol temple.
He struck the idols — crash! — and broke them all, except for the very largest. Then he left the big axe resting in its hands.

interaction.prompt: Tap Abraham's lesson to his people.

markers.label: Broken
markers.reveal: The broken idols showed they had no power.

markers.label: Big idol
markers.reveal: The largest idol could not answer a single question.

markers.label: Axe
markers.reveal: Abraham used the moment to wake up their thinking.

interaction.completeText: He wanted them to think, not just follow.

pages.text: When the people came back and saw the wreckage, they were furious. "Who did this to our gods?" they cried.
"Ask the big one!" Abraham said. "If it can speak, then it knows who broke the rest." But of course, the statue could say nothing at all.

section.title: The Fire That Could Not Burn

pages.text: The people were so angry that they built the biggest fire the world had ever seen — roaring, towering, hotter than any flame.
They cast Abraham into the very center of the fire. But Allah was watching over His friend.

quote.translation: We said, "O fire, be coolness and safety upon Abraham."

pages.text: The fire that was meant to hurt him became a garden. Cool grass grew under his feet. Flowers opened in the flame. Allah had turned danger into peace.
Abraham walked out unharmed, calm and unhurried. He had trusted Allah so completely that Allah called him Khalil — a beloved friend.

interaction.prompt: What made Abraham brave in front of the fire?

options.label: The fire was tiny.
options.feedback: The fire was terrifyingly large.

options.label: He trusted Allah.
options.feedback: Yes. Trust made him steady when danger was real.

options.label: The crowd helped him.
options.feedback: The crowd opposed him, but Allah protected him.

interaction.completeText: Courage is trust when the heat is real.

pages.text: A friend is someone you trust with everything. Abraham trusted Allah with his whole life, and Allah honored that trust with a miracle.

interaction.prompt: Tap how Allah changed danger into peace.

markers.label: Flames
markers.reveal: The fire was real, but it obeyed Allah.

markers.label: Garden
markers.reveal: Allah made coolness and safety in the very center of danger.

markers.label: Peace
markers.reveal: A miracle can turn fear into calm.

interaction.completeText: Even fire is a servant of Allah.

pages.text: After the fire, Abraham knew he could not stay in a town that chose stones over the truth. So he set out for a new land.
With him went his wife Hagar and their little son Ishmael. They travelled far, through heat and dust, trusting Allah every step.
Allah guided them to a quiet, empty valley — a dry place between bare hills, where no one lived yet. It seemed like nothing was there.
But Allah had chosen this very valley for something great — a house that would become the heart of the world.
Abraham and Ishmael began to build. Stone by stone, they raised a simple cube-shaped house — the Kaaba — just for worshipping Allah.

interaction.prompt: Tap what Abraham and Ishmael built together.

markers.label: Stones
markers.reveal: Stone by stone, they built for worship.

markers.label: Kaaba
markers.reveal: The Kaaba was raised for Allah alone.

markers.label: Prayer
markers.reveal: Their work was joined with dua.

interaction.completeText: The strongest buildings are raised with prayer.

pages.text: It was the very first house ever built only for Allah. No gold, no decoration — just honest stone and trusting hearts.
As they built, they prayed together. Father and son, side by side, raising stones and raising prayers — one for every layer.

quote.translation: Our Lord, accept this from us. Indeed, You are the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing.

pages.text: They asked Allah to make this a place of peace, a place where people would come from everywhere to pray and be close to Him.
And that is exactly what happened. Every year, millions of people visit the Kaaba, just as Abraham prayed they would.
Abraham teaches us to look carefully at the world and think. The biggest, brightest things in the sky are not God — they are only His signs.
He teaches us that sometimes standing alone for the truth is braver than following the crowd — even when the crowd is everyone you know.
He teaches us that when you trust Allah with everything, even fire cannot harm you. Trust is the rope that cannot break.
And he teaches us to build — not just houses of stone, but lives of prayer and kindness. That is what lasts.

interaction.prompt: Put Abraham's path in order.

items.label: He asks who made the world

interaction.completeText: Abraham's questions became a life of worship.

pages.text: Moses
In the land of Egypt ruled a king called Pharaoh. He was cruel and proud, and treated Allah's people terribly.
Pharaoh made them work without rest. He ordered that every baby boy born to them be taken away. It was a time of fear and sorrow.
But Allah was watching. Into those hard days, a baby boy was born — Moses. His mother loved him more than words could say.
To keep him safe, Allah placed a brave idea in his mother's heart: she gently set her baby in a little basket and let the river carry him away.

interaction.prompt: Tap the path Allah opened for baby Moses.

markers.label: Basket
markers.reveal: The basket carried a baby his mother loved deeply.

markers.label: River
markers.reveal: The river became a road by Allah's plan.

markers.label: Bank
markers.reveal: Allah promised the river would bring him where he needed to go.

interaction.completeText: Trust sometimes means placing what you love in Allah's care.

pages.text: She trusted Allah completely — even though it must have been the hardest thing she ever did. Trust sometimes means letting go.

interaction.prompt: What did Moses' mother show when she let the basket go?

options.label: Forgetting him.
options.feedback: She did not forget him. She loved him and trusted Allah.

options.label: Trust in Allah.
options.feedback: Yes. Trust can be the hardest kind of love.

options.label: Giving up hope.
options.feedback: She let go with hope in Allah's promise.

interaction.completeText: Her hands let go, but her hope held on.

quote.translation: ...cast him into the river, and the river will throw him onto the bank... Indeed, We will return him to you and make him one of the messengers.

pages.text: And where did the river carry him? Right to Pharaoh's own palace — where the little baby was found, loved, and raised as a prince.
Allah's plans are wiser than any king's. The river that was supposed to be dangerous became a road to safety.
Moses grew into a strong, fair young man. One day, traveling near a mountain in the dark, he saw a strange and beautiful fire glowing in the distance.
"Let me bring back a warming coal," he thought. But when he came close, a voice spoke from the fire — and it was not a human voice.
It was Allah Himself, speaking to Moses. Imagine that! Allah chose him, right there on that quiet mountain, to carry a great message to Pharaoh.

quote.translation: Indeed, I am Allah. There is no deity except Me, so worship Me and establish prayer for My remembrance.

arabic.meaning: Lord — the One who makes, owns, and lovingly cares for all things.

pages.text: Allah gave Moses wonderful signs — his staff could become a serpent, and his hand could shine bright as a lamp. These were proofs from Allah.

interaction.prompt: Tap the signs Allah gave Moses.

markers.label: Staff
markers.reveal: The staff became a sign by Allah's command.

markers.label: Light
markers.reveal: The shining hand was another proof from Allah.

markers.label: Proofs
markers.reveal: Signs were not magic tricks; they were truth made visible.

interaction.completeText: The signs pointed beyond Moses to Allah.

pages.text: But Pharaoh's heart was hard. Even when the signs were right in front of him, he refused to believe. A hard heart does not change by seeing — only by choosing.

interaction.prompt: Why did Pharaoh refuse even after seeing signs?

options.label: There were no signs.
options.feedback: The signs were right in front of him.

options.label: His heart chose pride.
options.feedback: Yes. Seeing is not enough if the heart refuses truth.

options.label: He was too kind.
options.feedback: Pharaoh was cruel and proud.

interaction.completeText: A soft heart matters more than a full argument.

pages.text: Moses warned Pharaoh again and again: "Let Allah's people go." But Pharaoh would not listen. He thought his power was greater than God's.
When someone keeps choosing wrong, consequences come. Allah sent signs of warning: the water turned bitter, the crops failed, the sky darkened.
At last, Moses led his people out of Egypt at night, walking toward freedom. They had waited a very long time for this moment.
But behind them thundered Pharaoh's army — chariots and soldiers in furious pursuit. And ahead of them stretched the wide, deep sea.
The people were terrified. The sea was in front, the army was behind — there was nowhere left to run. But Moses was not afraid.
"My Lord is with me," he said. "He will show the way." Sometimes the bravest thing you can say is: I trust Allah, even when I cannot see the way.

interaction.prompt: What did Moses say when danger was in front and behind?

options.label: We are finished.
options.feedback: The people were afraid, but Moses trusted Allah.

options.label: My Lord is with me.
options.feedback: Yes. Trust spoke before the road appeared.

options.label: Go back to Pharaoh.
options.feedback: Allah opened a way forward.

interaction.completeText: Trust can speak before the sea opens.

quote.translation: Then We inspired Moses, "Strike the sea with your staff," and it parted, and each portion was like a great towering mountain.

pages.text: Moses struck the sea with his staff — and the water rose up on either side like great mountains. A dry road opened through the very middle of the sea!

interaction.prompt: Tap the miracle at the sea.

markers.label: Staff
markers.reveal: Moses struck the sea only by Allah's command.

markers.label: Water walls
markers.reveal: The water stood like mountains.

markers.label: Dry path
markers.reveal: Allah made a road where there was none.

interaction.completeText: The danger became a doorway.

pages.text: Through the open sea they walked, safe between the walls of water. Every person crossed — every child, every elder. Allah had made a road where there was none.
When Pharaoh's army tried to follow, the walls of water came crashing down and the sea closed over them. The cruel king was gone.
On the far shore, Moses fell down in thanks to his Lord. He had trusted Allah through the fire, through the river, through the sea — and Allah had never let him down.
Moses teaches us that even when the world seems dark, Allah is watching. A river can carry a baby to safety. A fire can hold a sacred voice.
He teaches us that trust is not a feeling — it is a choice. Moses chose to trust even when the sea was in front and the army was behind.
He teaches us that a hard heart can never be satisfied — but a trusting heart finds miracles. The sea only parted because Moses chose to step forward.
And he teaches us that Allah's plans are always wiser than our fears. The river, the fire, the sea — they were never dangers. They were doors.

interaction.prompt: Put Moses' rescue signs in order.
interaction.completeText: River, fire, and sea became doors by Allah's plan.

chapters.title: Jonah
chapters.subtitle: The whale and the prayer in the dark

pages.text: Jonah
Jonah was a prophet who called his people to Allah. He warned them and taught them, but they would not listen.
Their refusal made him so sad and frustrated that he left — before Allah had told him to go. He walked away from the town on his own.

interaction.prompt: What was Jonah's mistake at the beginning?

options.label: He asked Allah for help.
options.feedback: Asking Allah is good.

options.label: He left too soon.
options.feedback: Yes. He walked away before Allah told him to go.

options.label: He warned his people.
options.feedback: Warning them was part of his prophetic work.

interaction.completeText: Patience means waiting for Allah's timing.

pages.text: Sometimes, when we are upset, we make decisions too quickly. Jonah's heart was heavy, and he acted before waiting for Allah's guidance.
He boarded a ship and sailed out across the open water. But the sky grew dark, the wind howled, and a fierce storm rose up around them.

interaction.prompt: Tap the storm Jonah entered.

markers.label: Clouds
markers.reveal: The sky darkened as the story turned.

markers.label: Waves
markers.reveal: The sea became wild and frightening.

markers.label: Ship
markers.reveal: The sailors knew they were in danger.

interaction.completeText: Running away did not bring Jonah peace.

pages.text: The sailors were afraid. The ship was filling with water. They drew lots to see who should go over the side — and Jonah's name was drawn.
Jonah knew this was because he had run away from what Allah asked him to do. So he told them to cast him into the sea, and they did.
Down, down he sank into the wild, dark sea — and a great whale opened its huge mouth and swallowed him whole.
Inside the whale it was dark — darker than night, darkness inside darkness inside the deep. Jonah was all alone.

interaction.prompt: Tap the layers of darkness around Jonah.

markers.label: Whale
markers.reveal: The whale became a hidden place for Jonah's turning back.

markers.label: Sea
markers.reveal: He was deep under the water.

markers.label: Darkness
markers.reveal: No darkness is too deep for Allah to hear.

interaction.completeText: Even there, Jonah was not beyond mercy.

pages.text: He had no one to talk to. No light, no company, no way out. But he was not truly alone — because Allah is always there, even in the darkest dark.
So Jonah did the most powerful thing a person can do: he turned to Allah and prayed from the very bottom of his heart.

interaction.prompt: What did Jonah do in the deepest dark?

options.label: He gave up.
options.feedback: He was afraid, but he did not give up.

options.label: He made dua.
options.feedback: Yes. He turned to Allah from the bottom of his heart.

options.label: He blamed the sailors.
options.feedback: He admitted his own wrong.

interaction.completeText: Dua works in places no one else can reach.

quote.translation: There is no deity except You; glory be to You. Indeed, I have been of the wrongdoers.

arabic.meaning: Calling on Allah from the heart — He hears it anywhere, even in the dark.

pages.text: Allah heard him — even there, in the deepest dark of the sea. No place is too far, no darkness too deep for Allah's mercy to reach.

quote.translation: So We responded to him and saved him from the distress. And thus do We save the believers.

pages.text: The whale set Jonah gently on the shore, weak and tired. The storm was gone, the sea was calm, and the morning light was warm.

interaction.prompt: Put Jonah's rescue in order.
interaction.completeText: The way out began with turning back.

pages.text: Allah grew a leafy plant to shade him and help him heal. Where there had been darkness, now there was shelter and care — mercy upon mercy.

interaction.prompt: Tap the mercies Allah gave Jonah.

markers.label: Shore
markers.reveal: Allah brought Jonah out of the sea alive.

markers.label: Shade
markers.reveal: The leafy plant sheltered him while he healed.

markers.label: Morning
markers.reveal: Warm light came after darkness.

interaction.completeText: Allah's mercy did not stop at rescue; it kept caring.

pages.text: When Jonah was well again, he returned to his people. And this time, every single one of them believed and turned to Allah.
Allah's mercy can reach anyone, anywhere. Even when we run away, even when we make mistakes, He is waiting for us to turn back.

interaction.prompt: Who can Allah's mercy reach?

options.label: Only people nearby.
options.feedback: Distance does not limit Allah.

options.label: Anyone who turns back.
options.feedback: Yes. Mercy reached Jonah and his whole town.

options.label: Only perfect people.
options.feedback: Mercy is for those who need Allah.

interaction.completeText: No one is too far to turn back.

pages.text: Jonah teaches us that we should never give up on people. His whole town believed in the end — it is never too late for someone to change.
He teaches us that du'a — sincere prayer from the heart — is the most powerful thing in the world. It works in the dark, in the deep, in the storm.
He teaches us patience: don't walk away before Allah tells you to go. And if you do walk away, He will still be there when you turn back.

chapters.title: Mary
chapters.subtitle: The palm tree and the birth of Jesus

pages.text: Mary
Mary was gentle and good from the time she was very small. She loved Allah dearly, and she spent her days praying and remembering Him.
Her mother had dedicated her to Allah even before she was born. She grew up inside the temple, cared for by the kind prophet Zachariah.

quote.translation: O Mary, indeed Allah has chosen you and purified you and chosen you above the women of the worlds.

pages.text: A wonderful thing happened: whenever Zachariah came to her, he found fresh fruit beside her — provision from Allah, given without any human hand.

interaction.prompt: Tap the provision Allah gave Mary.

markers.label: Fruit
markers.reveal: Fresh fruit came to Mary as rizq from Allah.

markers.label: Light
markers.reveal: The light reminds us that provision can arrive unexpectedly.

markers.label: Prayer place
markers.reveal: Mary's worship and trust filled this quiet place.

interaction.completeText: Allah can provide without any visible hand.

arabic.meaning: Provision — every good thing Allah gives, often in surprising ways.

pages.text: One day an angel came to Mary with astonishing news: Allah would give her a baby boy, even though no man had ever touched her. "How can this be?" she wondered.
The angel told her: for Allah, this is easy. When Allah wills a thing, He simply says to it, "Be," and it is.
She trusted Allah, even though she did not fully understand. Trusting Allah when you cannot see the whole plan is one of the bravest things a person can do.

interaction.prompt: What did Mary do when she could not see the whole plan?

options.label: She trusted Allah.
options.feedback: Yes. Trust can be brave and quiet.

options.label: She understood everything.
options.feedback: She trusted even without seeing everything.

options.label: She ran from Allah.
options.feedback: Mary stayed close to Allah.

interaction.completeText: Trust does not require seeing the whole road.

section.title: The Palm Tree and the Stream

pages.text: When her baby was about to come, Mary went away to a far, quiet place. She was alone, and afraid, and in pain. She rested against the trunk of a tall palm tree.
"I wish I had died before this," she whispered. She was in real pain — body and heart. But Allah had not forgotten her.

quote.translation: Do not grieve; your Lord has provided beneath you a stream. And shake toward you the trunk of the palm tree; it will drop upon you ripe, fresh dates.

interaction.prompt: Tap how Allah comforted Mary.

markers.label: Stream
markers.reveal: Cool water came beneath her when she needed relief.

markers.label: Dates
markers.reveal: Ripe dates fell from the palm by Allah's mercy.

markers.label: Palm
markers.reveal: The tree became a place of shelter and provision.

interaction.completeText: Comfort came in exactly the form she needed.

pages.text: A clear stream bubbled up to refresh her, and sweet ripe dates fell gently for her to eat. Allah comforted her exactly when she needed it most.
Mary carried her baby, Jesus, home. The people were unkind and full of questions. But Mary stayed calm and simply pointed to the child.
Then, by Allah's power, the tiny baby Jesus spoke from his cradle — and everyone fell silent in wonder.

quote.translation: He said, "Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet."

pages.text: Jesus was a prophet, too — sent by Allah, just like Abraham and Moses. He called people to worship only the One true God, and he performed miracles by Allah's leave.
Mary teaches us that Allah provides when we need it most. A stream in the desert, dates from a dry tree — His rizq comes in ways we never expect.

interaction.prompt: Put Mary's story in order.
interaction.completeText: Mary's path is full of trust and provision.

pages.text: She teaches us that trusting Allah is not about understanding everything — it is about knowing that His plan is always good, even when you cannot see it yet.

interaction.prompt: What is trust in Mary's story?

options.label: Knowing every detail.
options.feedback: Mary did not know every detail.

options.label: Knowing Allah's plan is good.
options.feedback: Yes. That is trust when the future is hidden.

options.label: Never feeling afraid.
options.feedback: Trust can live in a heart that feels afraid.

interaction.completeText: A trusting heart can still feel deeply.

pages.text: She teaches us that patience in pain brings comfort in the end. Mary was in real pain — and Allah's comfort came right on time. Never too early, never too late.

interaction.prompt: What should Mary help us remember when pain is real?

options.label: Allah forgot her.
options.feedback: Allah had not forgotten her at all.

options.label: Allah's comfort comes on time.
options.feedback: Yes. He knew exactly when and how to comfort her.

options.label: Her pain was not real.
options.feedback: Her pain was real, and Allah cared for her in it.

interaction.completeText: Real pain can still be held by mercy.

pages.text: Solomon
Solomon was a prophet and a king, and Allah gave him a wonderful gift: he could understand the speech of animals and birds!

interaction.prompt: Tap the gift Allah gave Solomon.

markers.label: Birds
markers.reveal: Solomon could understand the speech of birds.

markers.label: King
markers.reveal: He had power, but he stayed Allah's servant.

markers.label: Creation
markers.reveal: His gift helped him notice even small creatures.

interaction.completeText: A gift becomes beautiful when it makes the heart grateful.

pages.text: Imagine hearing what the birds say as they fly, what the ants whisper as they work, what the wind hums as it moves. This was Solomon's extraordinary gift from Allah.

quote.translation: And Solomon said, "O people, we have been taught the language of birds, and we have been given from all things. Indeed, this is evident bounty."

arabic.meaning: Wisdom — knowing the good and true thing to do.

pages.text: His kingdom was vast and wondrous. The wind, the birds, and many helpers served him by Allah's leave. Yet with all this power, Solomon stayed humble and thankful.
He knew that everything he had was a gift from Allah, not something he earned by himself. That knowledge kept his heart soft.

interaction.prompt: Why did Solomon stay humble with so much power?

options.label: He thought it was his alone.
options.feedback: That would have made pride grow.

options.label: He knew it came from Allah.
options.feedback: Yes. Knowing the source kept his heart soft.

options.label: He had no power.
options.feedback: He had great power, but used it with humility.

interaction.completeText: Remembering the Giver protects the gift.

pages.text: Power without humility becomes cruelty. But Solomon showed that the strongest person is the one who remembers where his strength comes from.
One day, Solomon's great army was marching along — until they came to a wide valley. It was the Valley of the Ants.
A tiny ant saw them coming and cried out to warn her friends: "Hurry, everyone — into your homes!"

interaction.prompt: Tap the tiny moment Solomon noticed.

markers.label: Ant
markers.reveal: A tiny ant cared enough to warn her people.

markers.label: Homes
markers.reveal: The ants had little dwellings worth protecting.

markers.label: Army
markers.reveal: A mighty army paused for a tiny creature.

interaction.completeText: Greatness can hear what is small.

quote.translation: O ants, enter your dwellings that you not be crushed by Solomon and his soldiers while they perceive not.

pages.text: Solomon heard the tiny ant's little voice — and he smiled. A mighty king, delighted by an ant! He stopped his whole army so the ants would be safe.

quote.translation: My Lord, enable me to be grateful for Your favor which You have bestowed upon me and upon my parents, and to do righteousness of which You approve.

pages.text: Solomon thanked Allah not just for the big gifts — the palace, the kingdom, the power — but even for being able to hear an ant. Every gift, no matter how small, deserves gratitude.

interaction.prompt: Tap gifts Solomon thanked Allah for.

markers.label: Birds
markers.reveal: Even understanding birds was a favor from Allah.

markers.label: Shade
markers.reveal: Small comforts are gifts too.

markers.label: Kingdom
markers.reveal: Power is a trust to use with justice.

interaction.completeText: Every gift, large or tiny, can become gratitude.

pages.text: He teaches us that even the very smallest creature matters. That is true greatness: to be strong, and gentle, and grateful, all at once.
Solomon teaches us that hikmah — wisdom — is a gift from Allah. Use it to be kind, not to be proud.

interaction.prompt: What is wisdom for?

options.label: To feel proud.
options.feedback: Wisdom with pride stops being wise.

options.label: To be kind and fair.
options.feedback: Yes. Wisdom should protect and serve.

options.label: To show off.
options.feedback: Solomon's wisdom led him to gratitude.

interaction.completeText: Wisdom shines when it serves.

pages.text: He teaches us that power without humility is dangerous, but power with gratitude is beautiful. The strongest king stopped his army for one tiny ant.

interaction.prompt: Put the Valley of the Ants story in order.
interaction.completeText: Power listened, paused, and became gratitude.

pages.text: He teaches us that every creature, no matter how small, deserves care and respect. Allah sees them all — and so should we.
Why Do Muslims Pray?
Five times every day — morning, midday, afternoon, evening, and night — Muslims stop whatever they are doing to spend a few quiet minutes talking with Allah. This is called Salah.

interaction.prompt: Tap the rhythm of the five quiet meetings.

markers.label: Morning
markers.reveal: Fajr begins the day by remembering Allah.

markers.label: Midday
markers.reveal: Prayer interrupts busyness with remembrance.

markers.label: Night
markers.reveal: The day closes by returning to Allah.

interaction.completeText: Salah gives the day a steady heartbeat.

arabic.meaning: Prayer — a loving daily meeting between a person and Allah.

pages.text: Imagine having someone you love, and five times a day you get to sit quietly and talk. That is what Salah is: not a burden, but a gift.

interaction.prompt: What is Salah in this chapter?

options.label: Only a burden.
options.feedback: The chapter calls it a gift, not just a duty.

options.label: A quiet meeting with Allah.
options.feedback: Yes. Salah is a loving pause with the One who hears.

options.label: A performance for people.
options.feedback: Prayer is for Allah, not showing off.

interaction.completeText: Prayer is a meeting, not a show.

pages.text: Before praying, Muslims wash — hands, face, arms, and feet — with cool, clean water. This is called wudu. Their bodies feel fresh, and their hearts feel calm and ready.

interaction.prompt: Tap what wudu prepares.

markers.label: Hands
markers.reveal: Hands are washed before standing before Allah.

markers.label: Water
markers.reveal: Clean water helps the body feel fresh.

markers.label: Heart
markers.reveal: Wudu also quiets the heart for prayer.

interaction.completeText: Clean water prepares more than skin.

pages.text: Wudu is a way of preparing your body and your heart. You wash away the dust of the day so you can stand before Allah clean and calm.
Then, wherever they are in the whole wide world, Muslims turn to face one special place — the Kaaba in Makkah — so that everyone faces together, like one enormous family.
A beautiful call rings out, called the adhan. It sings, "Allahu akbar" — "Allah is the Greatest" — inviting everyone to come and pray.
In prayer, Muslims stand, then bow, then rest their forehead gently on the ground. Placing your forehead on the floor for Allah is the closest and most loving a person can be to Him.

interaction.prompt: Tap the prayer postures.

markers.label: Stand
markers.reveal: Standing begins prayer with attention.

markers.label: Bow
markers.reveal: Bowing teaches humility before Allah.

markers.label: Sujud
markers.reveal: Prostration is the closest posture of love.

interaction.completeText: The whole body learns worship.

quote.translation: Indeed, I am Allah... so worship Me and establish prayer for My remembrance.

pages.text: In those quiet minutes, Muslims thank Allah, ask Him for help, and tell Him what is in their hearts. Worries grow lighter, and a gentle peace settles inside.

quote.translation: Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah hearts are assured.

pages.text: Praying is a little like watering a small plant in your heart, five times a day — so your love for Allah keeps growing greener and stronger.
And anyone can pray — even a child, even you. You only need a clean little spot, a quiet moment, and an open heart. Allah is always ready to listen.

interaction.prompt: Who can begin learning to pray?

options.label: Only grown-ups.
options.feedback: Children can begin learning too.

options.label: Even a child.
options.feedback: Yes. A clean spot and open heart are enough to begin.

options.label: Only perfect people.
options.feedback: Prayer is how imperfect people return to Allah.

interaction.completeText: You can begin with one sincere step.

pages.text: Prayer teaches us that we are never truly alone. No matter where you are, when you pray, you are standing before the One who made you and loves you.
It teaches us discipline — stopping five times a day reminds us that Allah matters more than anything else we could be doing.
And it teaches us that peace does not come from having everything perfect — it comes from knowing that Allah is with you, right now, in this moment.

interaction.prompt: Where does prayer's peace come from?

options.label: Everything being perfect.
options.feedback: Life does not need to be perfect for the heart to find Allah.

options.label: Knowing Allah is with you.
options.feedback: Yes. That knowledge settles the heart.

options.label: Being seen by people.
options.feedback: Salah is a quiet meeting with Allah.

interaction.completeText: Peace can arrive right where you are.

interaction.prompt: Put these prayer movements in order.
interaction.completeText: The movements become a path of remembrance.

chapters.title: Why Do Many Muslim Women Cover?
chapters.subtitle: A choice made with love

pages.text: Why Do Many Muslim Women Cover?
You may have seen women wearing a soft scarf over their hair. It is called hijab. Many Muslim women choose to wear it — and they wear it with pride and joy.

interaction.prompt: Tap what the scarf can remind us of.

markers.label: Scarf
markers.reveal: Hijab can be a visible act of worship.

markers.label: Joy
markers.reveal: Many Muslim women wear it with pride and joy.

markers.label: Dignity
markers.reveal: The feeling is dignified, not hidden or ashamed.

interaction.completeText: The outside sign points to an inside intention.

arabic.meaning: A covering — also a gentle way of guarding what is precious.

pages.text: Hijab is not just a scarf. It is a whole idea: living with modesty, dignity, and grace — something Allah asks of both men and women.

interaction.prompt: What is hijab in this chapter?

options.label: Only a scarf.
options.feedback: The scarf matters, but hijab is bigger than cloth.

options.label: Modesty, dignity, and grace.
options.feedback: Yes. It is a whole way of living before Allah.

options.label: A sign of shame.
options.feedback: The chapter says it is never out of shame.

interaction.completeText: Hijab begins with dignity before Allah.

pages.text: Allah lovingly asks both men and women to be modest — to dress and to act with dignity, gentleness, and respect for themselves and others.

quote.translation: And tell the believing women... to draw their head-coverings over their chests, and not to display their beauty except to their families...

pages.text: Covering does not mean a woman must hide. It means that what is most beautiful about her — her mind, her heart, her kindness — is for everyone, while her beauty is hers to share as she chooses.

interaction.prompt: What does covering not mean?

options.label: A woman must hide.
options.feedback: Correct. Covering does not mean her mind and kindness disappear.

options.label: She has dignity.
options.feedback: Dignity is part of the meaning, not the wrong answer.

options.label: She can worship Allah.
options.feedback: Worship is part of why many women cover.

interaction.completeText: A woman is known by her heart, mind, and kindness.

pages.text: It is a little like a treasured gift kept carefully wrapped: precious, protected, and given freely — never grabbed or taken.

interaction.prompt: Tap the gift picture and what it teaches.

markers.label: Wrap
markers.reveal: The wrap shows care, not shame.

markers.label: Ribbon
markers.reveal: Beauty can be guarded with dignity.

markers.label: Gift
markers.reveal: A person's worth is precious with or without being displayed.

interaction.completeText: The picture is about honor and care.

quote.translation: ...that is more suitable that they will be recognized and not be harmed. And ever is Allah Forgiving and Merciful.

arabic.meaning: A gentle modesty and dignity that lives in the heart.

pages.text: When a girl chooses to cover, she is saying, with love: "I want to be known for who I truly am — and I do this for Allah." It is never out of shame. She is honored.
Some Muslim women cover, and some do not, and they do it in many different and beautiful ways. What matters most to Allah is the same in everyone: a kind, honest, and modest heart.

interaction.prompt: Tap the variety this page protects.

markers.label: Different ways
markers.reveal: Muslim women dress modestly in many beautiful styles.

markers.label: Respect
markers.reveal: The reader learns to speak respectfully, not judge.

markers.label: Heart
markers.reveal: Allah sees the heart most of all.

interaction.completeText: The lesson is dignity without judging people.

pages.text: Haya' — modesty — lives in the heart first. A kind word, a gentle look, a humble spirit — these are the real coverings that Allah treasures.

interaction.prompt: Where does haya live first?

options.label: In the heart.
options.feedback: Yes. Modesty begins inside before it appears outside.

options.label: Only in a mirror.
options.feedback: The mirror cannot measure the heart.

options.label: In being noticed.
options.feedback: Haya is humble, not attention-seeking.

interaction.completeText: The most important covering is a beautiful heart.

pages.text: Mary, the mother of Jesus, is one of the most honored women in all of Islam. She covered herself with modesty and devotion — and Allah chose her above the women of all the worlds.

interaction.prompt: Put the hijab lesson in order.
interaction.completeText: The lesson ends with dignity and respect.

chapters.title: The Month of Ramadan
chapters.subtitle: A month of fasting, light, and giving

pages.text: The Month of Ramadan
Once each year comes a very special month called Ramadan — the month when Allah first began to send down the Qur'an to the Prophet Muhammad.

interaction.prompt: Tap signs that Ramadan has arrived.

markers.label: Crescent
markers.reveal: The new moon marks the special month.

markers.label: Lanterns
markers.reveal: Light and joy fill many homes at Ramadan.

markers.label: Qur'an
markers.reveal: Ramadan is the month when the Qur'an first began to come down.

interaction.completeText: Ramadan begins with moonlight and revelation.

quote.translation: The month of Ramadan in which was revealed the Qur'an, a guidance for the people and clear proofs of guidance and the criterion...

arabic.meaning: Fasting — choosing not to eat or drink for a time, to please Allah.

pages.text: From the first light of dawn until the sun goes down, grown-ups — and many older children — do not eat or drink at all. This is called fasting, or sawm.
Before dawn they share a quiet little meal called suhoor. Then, through the day, they feel what it is like to be hungry — and they remember all the people who do not have enough.

interaction.prompt: Tap the early-morning Ramadan lesson.

markers.label: Suhoor
markers.reveal: The quiet meal before dawn prepares the fasting day.

markers.label: Dawn
markers.reveal: Fasting begins when dawn's first light comes.

markers.label: Compassion
markers.reveal: Hunger helps the heart remember people who need food.

interaction.completeText: The day begins with intention and compassion.

pages.text: Fasting is not only about the tummy. It also means keeping your eyes, your tongue, and your hands kind, gentle, and clean all day long.

interaction.prompt: Fasting is not only about which part of you?

options.label: Only the tummy.
options.feedback: Correct. The tongue, eyes, hands, and heart fast too.

options.label: Kindness.
options.feedback: Kindness is part of fasting well.

options.label: Self-control.
options.feedback: Self-control is one of Ramadan's gifts.

interaction.completeText: A fasting heart tries to become gentler.

pages.text: By feeling hunger yourself, you understand what it is like for people who are hungry every day. Fasting grows compassion in your heart.

interaction.prompt: Why does fasting let a person feel hunger?

options.label: So they can complain.
options.feedback: Ramadan teaches patience, not complaint.

options.label: To grow compassion.
options.feedback: Yes. Feeling hunger helps us remember those who have less.

options.label: To ignore others.
options.feedback: Fasting should make us more generous.

interaction.completeText: Compassion grows when the heart understands.
