pages.text: When the sun finally sets, everyone gathers to break the fast together — often with a sweet date and a sip of cool water first. This happy meal is called iftar.

interaction.prompt: Tap how the fast opens at sunset.

markers.label: Date
markers.reveal: Many people break fast first with a sweet date.

markers.label: Water
markers.reveal: A sip of water feels like a mercy after the day.

markers.label: Together
markers.reveal: Iftar gathers hearts around gratitude.

interaction.completeText: The first bites after patience taste like thanks.

pages.text: At night, people pray a little longer, read the Qur'an, and give extra food and money to those in need. Hearts grow soft and generous all month.
Hidden in the last nights of Ramadan is a single, secret, precious night — the Night of Decree — which Allah says is better than a thousand months.

quote.translation: The Night of Decree is better than a thousand months.

pages.text: When Ramadan ends, there is a wonderful celebration called Eid! New clothes, sweet treats, visits with family, gifts for children — and a big thank-you to Allah.
Eid is a reminder: after patience comes joy. After effort comes ease. After fasting comes feasting. Allah's pattern is always the same.

interaction.prompt: What does Eid remind us after Ramadan?

options.label: There is no joy.
options.feedback: Eid is full of joy and gratitude.

options.label: After patience comes joy.
options.feedback: Yes. Allah teaches effort, then ease.

options.label: Forget Allah.
options.feedback: Eid is a big thank-you to Allah.

interaction.completeText: Patience and joy belong together.

pages.text: Ramadan teaches us that we are stronger than we think. If you can go a whole day without food for Allah, you can do hard things with His help.
It teaches us that compassion grows when we share the feeling of hunger. We remember those who have less, and we give more.
And it teaches us that patience and self-control are gifts — they make us kinder, humbler, and closer to Allah.

interaction.prompt: Put Ramadan's rhythm in order.
interaction.completeText: The month moves from intention to joy.

chapters.title: The Joy of Giving
chapters.subtitle: Why kindness is a kind of treasure

pages.text: The Joy of Giving
In Islam, giving is a kind of happiness. Helping others, sharing what you have, and being kind all have a beautiful name: sadaqah.

arabic.meaning: A gift given freely to help others and to please Allah.

pages.text: Sadaqah does not have to be money at all. Even a warm smile, a kind word, or moving a stone out of someone's path is sadaqah.

interaction.prompt: Tap forms of sadaqah that are not money.

markers.label: Clear path
markers.reveal: Moving harm from a path can be sadaqah.

markers.label: Stone
markers.reveal: Even a small removed stone can protect someone.

markers.label: Kind word
markers.reveal: A gentle word can be a gift to Allah.

interaction.completeText: Kindness has many shapes.

pages.text: The Prophet Muhammad taught that every single act of kindness is a charity. Imagine — your smile can be a gift to Allah!

interaction.prompt: Which small action can be sadaqah?

options.label: A warm smile.
options.feedback: Yes. A smile can be a gift when it is sincere.

options.label: Showing off.
options.feedback: Showing off harms the intention.

options.label: Hurting feelings.
options.feedback: Sadaqah brings kindness, not harm.

interaction.completeText: Small kindness can be real charity.

quote.translation: The example of those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah is like a seed that grows seven ears, in each ear a hundred grains. And Allah multiplies for whom He wills.

interaction.prompt: Tap the picture of a gift growing.

markers.label: Seed
markers.reveal: A small gift can begin like one tiny seed.

markers.label: Seven ears
markers.reveal: Allah can multiply goodness far beyond what we see.

markers.label: Grain
markers.reveal: Every grain reminds us that Allah grows sincere giving.

interaction.completeText: What is small for us is not small with Allah.

pages.text: That is Allah's beautiful promise: a small gift given with love grows and grows, far bigger than a large gift given with pride. Allah returns it many, many times over.
The secret is the heart behind the gift. Allah does not look at the size of what you give — He looks at the love with which you give it.

interaction.prompt: What does Allah look at in sadaqah?

options.label: Only the size.
options.feedback: A big gift with pride can be smaller than a small gift with love.

options.label: The love behind it.
options.feedback: Yes. The heart behind the gift matters most.

options.label: How many people saw it.
options.feedback: Being seen is not the goal.

interaction.completeText: A sincere heart makes the gift shine.

pages.text: We can share food with a neighbor, care for a hungry animal, comfort someone who is sad, or give a toy to a child who has less. Every little kindness counts.

interaction.prompt: Tap people or needs sadaqah can touch.

markers.label: Food
markers.reveal: Sharing food can fill a stomach and a heart.

markers.label: Neighbor
markers.reveal: A neighbor's door can become a place of kindness.

markers.label: Home
markers.reveal: Sadaqah can quietly strengthen a whole community.

interaction.completeText: Every little kindness counts.

pages.text: And here is the secret: the more you give, the bigger your heart grows. Often the one who gives feels even happier than the one who receives.
Giving in secret is even more beautiful. When you give without anyone knowing, it is just between you and Allah — and that is the purest kind of giving.

interaction.prompt: Why is giving in secret beautiful?

options.label: It brings fame.
options.feedback: Secret giving is not chasing fame.

options.label: It is between you and Allah.
options.feedback: Yes. That keeps the intention pure.

options.label: It makes the gift smaller.
options.feedback: A hidden gift can be huge with Allah.

interaction.completeText: Hidden goodness is still seen by Allah.

pages.text: Giving teaches us that everything we have is a gift from Allah — and gifts are meant to be shared, not hoarded.
It teaches us that happiness does not come from having the most — it comes from giving the best of yourself. A kind word, a patient ear, a helping hand.
And it teaches us that Allah's promise is true: what you give for His sake never, ever goes to waste. It comes back to you in ways you cannot imagine.

interaction.prompt: Put the sadaqah path in order.
interaction.completeText: Giving grows when the heart is sincere.

chapters.title: Kindness to Parents and Family
chapters.subtitle: How we love the people closest to us

pages.text: Kindness to Parents and Family
Right after loving Allah, He asks us to do something close to home: to be kind, gentle, and loving to our parents.

quote.translation: ...and to parents, good treatment. Whether one or both of them reach old age with you, say not to them even 'uff,' and do not repel them, but speak to them a noble word.

pages.text: That tiny word "uff" is the smallest little grumble — and Allah asks us not even to sigh in annoyance at our parents. Instead, we choose soft and gentle words.

interaction.prompt: What does the tiny word uff teach?

options.label: Even small grumbles matter.
options.feedback: Yes. Allah teaches us to guard even little signs of annoyance.

options.label: Harsh words are best.
options.feedback: The verse teaches noble, gentle words.

options.label: Ignore parents.
options.feedback: Kindness begins by paying attention.

interaction.completeText: Gentleness can begin with one small word held back.

pages.text: Allah does not ask for something impossible — He asks for kindness. And kindness is simply choosing gentle words when you could choose harsh ones.

interaction.prompt: When you could choose harsh words, what does kindness choose?

options.label: Shouting.
options.feedback: Shouting usually makes hearts harder.

options.label: Gentle words.
options.feedback: Yes. Kindness often starts with the voice.

options.label: Mocking.
options.feedback: Mocking is not birr.

interaction.completeText: A soft answer can protect the whole room.

quote.translation: And lower to them the wing of humility out of mercy, and say, "My Lord, have mercy upon them as they brought me up when I was small."

arabic.meaning: Loving goodness — especially the deep kindness we show our parents.

pages.text: Our parents cared for us when we were tiny and could do nothing for ourselves — they fed us, held us, and stayed awake for us. Our kindness is how we say thank you.

interaction.prompt: Tap ways parents care when children are small.

markers.label: Holding
markers.reveal: Parents held us when we could not hold ourselves up.

markers.label: Feeding
markers.reveal: They fed us before we could feed ourselves.

markers.label: Staying awake
markers.reveal: Love often looks like losing sleep for someone small.

interaction.completeText: Remembering care makes gratitude easier.

pages.text: Birr is the word for that deep, loving goodness — the kind that does not count what it gives, but simply gives because it loves.
And family is a gift from Allah — brothers and sisters, grandparents, aunts and uncles. We forgive each other, share with each other, and stay close.
The Prophet Muhammad taught that the best people are the ones who are kindest to their own families. Love begins at home.
Even a small act of kindness at home — making tea for someone, helping with a chore, saying "thank you" — is beloved to Allah.

interaction.prompt: Tap small acts that count at home.

markers.label: Tea
markers.reveal: Making tea can be love in a cup.

markers.label: Helping
markers.reveal: Helping with a chore is worship when done for Allah.

markers.label: Thank you
markers.reveal: A grateful word can warm the whole home.

interaction.completeText: Home is where kindness gets practiced every day.

section.title: What Kindness at Home Teaches Us

pages.text: Kindness at home teaches us that the people closest to us deserve our best, not our leftovers. The ones who see us every day should see our gentleness.
It teaches us that forgiveness is stronger than anger. Families are not perfect, but love and patience can heal almost anything.

interaction.prompt: What is stronger than anger in a family?

options.label: Holding a grudge.
options.feedback: Grudges make love feel far away.

options.label: Forgiveness and patience.
options.feedback: Yes. They help love heal what anger hurts.

options.label: Always winning.
options.feedback: Family is not a contest to win.

interaction.completeText: Forgiveness is strength at home.

pages.text: And it teaches us that the way we treat our family is the way we learn to treat the world. Kindness at home becomes kindness everywhere.

interaction.prompt: Put a kind family response in order.
interaction.completeText: Kindness at home can become kindness everywhere.

chapters.title: What Allah Promises Those Who Love Him
chapters.subtitle: The end of our journey

pages.text: What Allah Promises
Through all of these stories, one thing shines like the sun: Allah never, ever forgets the ones who turn to Him.
From Adam's mistake to Noah's flood, from Abraham's fire to Moses' sea, from Jonah's whale to Mary's palm tree — every story tells the same truth: Allah is always there.
He promises that He is always near — nearer than you can imagine. Whenever you call on Him, He hears you.

interaction.prompt: What does Allah promise when you call on Him?

options.label: He is near and hears.
options.feedback: Yes. This promise runs through the whole book.

options.label: He is gone.
options.feedback: Allah is never absent or unaware.

options.label: He is too busy.
options.feedback: Allah is never too busy for His servants.

interaction.completeText: Nearness is one of the promises to carry with you.

quote.translation: And when My servants ask you concerning Me — indeed I am near. I respond to the call of the caller when he calls upon Me.

pages.text: He promises that no sadness lasts forever. With every hard thing, Allah sends ease — gently, surely, again and again.

interaction.prompt: Tap signs that ease can come after hardship.

markers.label: Clouds
markers.reveal: Hardship can feel heavy like storm clouds.

markers.label: Light
markers.reveal: Allah can open light through what felt closed.

markers.label: Green valley
markers.reveal: Ease can grow after rain and difficulty.

interaction.completeText: Hardship does not get the last word.

quote.translation: For indeed, with hardship comes ease. Indeed, with hardship comes ease.

pages.text: Notice: Allah says it twice. With hardship comes ease. He repeats it because He knows we need to hear it again. Hardship does not have the last word — ease does.
And He promises that no one who is sorry and turns back to Him is ever turned away. Allah's mercy is bigger than any mistake.

interaction.prompt: What does Allah promise the one who turns back sorry?

options.label: Always turned away.
options.feedback: Allah does not turn away sincere tawbah.

options.label: Mercy is open.
options.feedback: Yes. His mercy is bigger than any mistake.

options.label: Be perfect first.
options.feedback: We return because we need mercy.

interaction.completeText: Hope stays open.

quote.translation: Do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed, Allah forgives all sins...

pages.text: Adam turned back and was forgiven. Jonah turned back and was saved. Every prophet who stumbled turned to Allah and found Him waiting with open arms.

arabic.meaning: Paradise — a home of endless joy and peace with Allah.

pages.text: To everyone who believes and does good, Allah promises a beautiful home forever — where every good thing waits, and no one is ever afraid or sad again.

interaction.prompt: Tap what Jannah means in this promise.

markers.label: Rivers
markers.reveal: The garden is described with beauty and peace.

markers.label: Trees
markers.reveal: Every good thing there is from Allah's mercy.

markers.label: No fear
markers.reveal: Allah promises a home where sadness and fear end.

interaction.completeText: Jannah is hope made forever.

pages.text: And best of all, He promises that they will be loved by Allah, and one day will see the One who made them, and loved them, all along.

section.title: The Beginning of the Rest of Your Life

pages.text: So wherever you are, little one, you can always talk to Allah. He is listening. He loves you. And He is the Most Merciful, the Most Kind.

quote.translation: In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful.

interaction.prompt: Put the book's big lesson in order.
interaction.completeText: That is a path you can keep walking.

pages.text: This is not the end of the story. It is the beginning of your story. Every prophet, every lesson, every promise — they were all leading here, to you.

interaction.prompt: What does the book ask the reader to carry forward?

options.label: Forget every lesson.
options.feedback: The stories are meant to travel with you.

options.label: Begin your own story with Allah.
options.feedback: Yes. The ending opens into your life.

options.label: Only watch from far away.
options.feedback: These lessons are for your real days.

interaction.completeText: The book closes by opening a door.

interaction.prompt: Tap the worlds gathered in the final picture.

markers.label: Mountains
markers.reveal: Moses, Abraham, and many stories stood near mountains.

markers.label: Sea
markers.reveal: Noah, Moses, and Jonah all learned that water obeys Allah.

markers.label: Road
markers.reveal: The last page invites your own next step.

interaction.completeText: All the lessons gather into a hopeful beginning.
